docs.title: @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance
docs.text: Zero-tolerance ESLint plugin and config for enforcing strict code quality standards in TypeScript projects.

docs.title: What is this?
docs.text:
@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance is a collection of custom ESLint rules designed to enforce a zero-tolerance policy on common TypeScript code quality issues. The rules cover:

  • Naming conventions — prefix interfaces with I, sort imports and functions alphabetically
  • Documentation — require JSDoc on all functions, require .describe() on Zod schemas
  • Testing standards — enforce should-prefixed descriptions, ban imprecise Jest matchers and leaky mocks
  • Type safety — ban as assertions, non-null ! operators, literal union types, and banned utility types
  • Code quality — limit function length and parameter count, eliminate magic numbers and strings
  • Error handling — require new Error() in throw statements, disallow empty catch blocks
  • Import hygiene — ban parent-relative imports, dynamic imports, and export aliases
  • Bug prevention — flag identical expressions, redundant boolean comparisons, and await inside loops

docs.title: Packages
docs.text:
This monorepo publishes two packages:

Package Description @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance The ESLint plugin with all custom rules @coderrob/eslint-config-zero-tolerance Pre-built recommended and strict config presets

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
npm install --save-dev @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance

// eslint.config.js (ESLint 9+ Flat Config)
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [zeroTolerance.configs.recommended];

See the Getting Started guide for full setup instructions and the Rules reference for descriptions of every rule.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance ships with four built-in config presets so you can go from zero to linting in one line.

docs.title: Preset Overview
docs.text: Preset Severity Use case recommended warn Gradual adoption; shows violations without breaking the build strict error New projects or full zero-tolerance enforcement; fails on any violation legacy-recommended warn ESLint 8 .eslintrc format legacy-strict error ESLint 8 .eslintrc format

docs.title: recommended
docs.text:
All rules enabled at warn. Function body limit: 20 lines. Parameter limit: 4.

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [zeroTolerance.configs.recommended];

docs.title: strict
docs.text:
All rules enabled at error. Function body limit: 10 lines. Parameter limit: 4.

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [zeroTolerance.configs.strict];

docs.title: legacy-recommended
legacy-strict
Rules Included in Each Preset
docs.text:
All rules are included in every preset. The only difference between recommended and strict is the severity level and the tighter limits for configurable rules.

Rule Recommended Strict require-interface-prefix warn error require-test-description-style warn error require-zod-schema-description warn error require-jsdoc-functions warn error no-magic-numbers warn error no-magic-strings warn error no-banned-types warn error no-dynamic-import warn error no-literal-unions warn error no-export-alias warn error no-jest-have-been-called warn error no-mock-implementation warn error no-type-assertion warn error no-eslint-disable warn error sort-imports warn error sort-functions warn error max-function-lines warn (max: 20) error (max: 10) max-params warn (max: 4) error (max: 4) no-identical-expressions warn error no-redundant-boolean warn error no-empty-catch warn error no-non-null-assertion warn error no-await-in-loop warn error no-throw-literal warn error no-re-export warn error require-optional-chaining warn error

docs.title: Disabling Individual Rules
docs.text:
Override any rule to off inside your config:

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [
  zeroTolerance.configs.recommended,
  {
    rules: {
      // Disable rules that don't suit your project
      'zero-tolerance/require-zod-schema-description': 'off',
      'zero-tolerance/sort-functions': 'off',
    },
  },
];

docs.title: Configuring Rule Options
docs.text: Two rules accept an options object:

docs.title: max-function-lines
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': ['warn', { max: 50 }]

docs.title: max-params
docs.text: 'zero-tolerance/max-params': ['error', { max: 3 }]

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide shows how to add @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance to a TypeScript project from scratch.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: Dependency Version Node.js 18+ TypeScript 5.x ESLint 8.57+ or 9.x @typescript-eslint/parser 8.x

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
npmpnpmyarn 
npm install --save-dev @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance @typescript-eslint/parser

pnpm add -D @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance @typescript-eslint/parser

yarn add -D @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance @typescript-eslint/parser

docs.title: Recommended preset
docs.text:
The recommended preset enables all rules at warn severity, making it easy to adopt incrementally.

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [
  zeroTolerance.configs.recommended,
  // your other configs...
];

docs.title: Strict preset
docs.text:
The strict preset enables all rules at error severity and applies tighter limits (e.g. max function body: 10 lines).

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [zeroTolerance.configs.strict];

docs.title: Custom configuration
docs.text:
Pick individual rules and set severities yourself:

eslint.config.js
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default [
  {
    plugins: {
      'zero-tolerance': zeroTolerance,
    },
    rules: {
      'zero-tolerance/require-interface-prefix': 'error',
      'zero-tolerance/no-throw-literal': 'error',
      'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': ['warn', { max: 40 }],
      'zero-tolerance/max-params': ['warn', { max: 5 }],
      // ... add any rules you need
    },
  },
];

docs.title: Using the separate config package
docs.text:
If you prefer to import pre-built config presets as separate modules:

npm install --save-dev @coderrob/eslint-config-zero-tolerance
eslint.config.js
import recommended from '@coderrob/eslint-config-zero-tolerance/recommended';
// or
import strict from '@coderrob/eslint-config-zero-tolerance/strict';

export default [
  recommended, // or strict
];

docs.title: ESLint 8.x (Legacy .eslintrc)
Typical TypeScript Project Setup
docs.text:
A complete eslint.config.js for a TypeScript project:

eslint.config.js
import js from '@eslint/js';
import tseslint from 'typescript-eslint';
import zeroTolerance from '@coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance';

export default tseslint.config(
  js.configs.recommended,
  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,
  zeroTolerance.configs.recommended,
  {
    files: ['src/**/*.ts'],
    rules: {
      // Override individual rules as needed
      'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': ['warn', { max: 50 }],
    },
  },
  {
    // Relax rules in test files
    files: ['**/*.test.ts', '**/*.spec.ts'],
    rules: {
      'zero-tolerance/require-jsdoc-functions': 'off',
      'zero-tolerance/no-type-assertion': 'off',
    },
  },
);

docs.title: Configuring tsconfig.json
docs.text:
No TypeScript-specific configuration is required by this plugin. All rules operate purely on the AST without requiring type information.

tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "target": "ES2020",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler"
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Browse the Rules reference for descriptions, examples, and severity settings for every rule.
  • Read the Configuration page to understand the difference between the recommended and strict presets.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text: @coderrob/eslint-plugin-zero-tolerance provides a suite of custom rules grouped into seven categories.

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text: Rule Type Description require-interface-prefix suggestion Enforce that interface names start with I

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: Rule Type Description require-jsdoc-functions suggestion Require JSDoc comments on all functions (except test files) require-optional-chaining suggestion Require optional chaining instead of repeated guard access require-zod-schema-description suggestion Enforce that Zod schemas have .describe() called

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: Rule Type Description require-test-description-style suggestion Enforce that test descriptions start with should no-jest-have-been-called suggestion Prohibit imprecise call-assertion matchers; use toHaveBeenCalledTimes and toHaveBeenNthCalledWith instead no-mock-implementation suggestion Prohibit persistent mock methods; use Once variants to prevent test bleeds

docs.title: Type Safety
docs.text: Rule Type Description no-type-assertion suggestion Prevent use of TypeScript as type assertions no-non-null-assertion problem Disallow non-null assertions using the ! postfix operator no-literal-unions suggestion Ban literal union types in favour of enums no-banned-types problem Ban ReturnType and indexed access types

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text: Rule Type Description max-function-lines suggestion Enforce a maximum number of lines per function body max-params suggestion Enforce a maximum number of function parameters no-magic-numbers suggestion Disallow magic numbers; use named constants instead no-magic-strings suggestion Disallow magic strings in comparisons and switch cases sort-imports suggestion Require import declarations to be ordered by group and alphabetically within each group sort-functions suggestion Require top-level functions and const function expressions to be sorted alphabetically

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Rule Type Description no-empty-catch problem Disallow empty catch blocks that silently swallow errors no-throw-literal problem Disallow throwing literals, objects, or templates; always throw a new Error instance

docs.title: Imports
docs.text: Rule Type Description no-dynamic-import problem Ban await import() and require() outside test files no-export-alias suggestion Prevent use of aliases in export statements no-re-export suggestion Disallow re-export statements from parent/grandparent modules

docs.title: Bug Prevention
docs.text: Rule Type Description no-identical-expressions problem Disallow identical expressions on both sides of a binary or logical operator no-redundant-boolean suggestion Disallow redundant comparisons to boolean literals no-await-in-loop problem Disallow await inside loops; use Promise.all() instead no-eslint-disable suggestion Prevent use of eslint-disable comments

docs.title: max-function-lines
docs.text: Enforce a maximum number of lines per function body.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn (max: 20) Strict error (max: 10)

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Long functions are harder to understand, test, and maintain. Keeping functions small forces a single-responsibility design: each function does one thing, has a clear name, and can be tested in isolation. This rule counts the lines of a function's block body (inclusive of opening and closing braces) and reports any function that exceeds the configured limit. Arrow functions with concise expression bodies (no block) are not checked.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
/**
 * Validates a user registration payload.
 */
function validateRegistration(payload: IRegistrationPayload): IValidationResult {
  const errors: string[] = [];
  if (!payload.email) errors.push('Email is required');
  if (!payload.password) errors.push('Password is required');
  return { valid: errors.length === 0, errors };
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect (exceeds limit)
docs.text:
function doEverything(user: IUser) {
  // 35+ lines of mixed concerns...
}

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Option Type Default Description max number 30 Maximum allowed lines in a function body

The standalone default is 30 lines. The recommended preset overrides this to 20 and the strict preset to 10.

// Use the recommended preset value (20 lines)
'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': 'warn'

// Custom limit
'zero-tolerance/max-function-lines': ['error', { max: 50 }]

docs.title: max-params
docs.text: Enforce a maximum number of function parameters.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn (max: 4) Strict error (max: 4)

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Functions with many parameters are hard to call correctly, difficult to read, and a sign that the function is doing too much or that related parameters should be grouped into a structured options object. Limiting the parameter count encourages better API design.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
/**
 * Creates a new user record.
 */
function createUser(options: ICreateUserOptions): Promise<IUser> {
  return db.users.create(options);
}

/**
 * Sends an email notification.
 */
function sendEmail(to: string, subject: string, body: string): Promise<void> {
  return mailer.send({ to, subject, body });
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function createUser(
  name: string,
  email: string,
  password: string,
  role: string,
  createdAt: Date,
): Promise<IUser> {
  // Too many positional parameters
}

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Option Type Default Description max number 4 Maximum number of allowed parameters 
// Default (max 4)
'zero-tolerance/max-params': 'warn'

// Custom limit
'zero-tolerance/max-params': ['error', { max: 3 }]

docs.title: no-await-in-loop
docs.text: Disallow await expressions inside loop bodies; use Promise.all() or Promise.allSettled() instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
await inside a loop serialises asynchronous operations: each iteration waits for the previous one to finish before starting the next. This is almost never the intended behaviour and is a significant performance bottleneck. Promise.all() runs the operations in parallel, completing in the time of the slowest single operation rather than the sum of all operations.

The rule detects await inside for, for...in, for...of, while, and do...while loops. It does not flag await inside callbacks or async functions nested within the loop body.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Run all requests in parallel
const results = await Promise.all(ids.map((id) => fetchUser(id)));

// Settle all, collecting errors
const settled = await Promise.allSettled(items.map((item) => process(item)));

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
for (const id of ids) {
  // Serialised — each request waits for the previous
  const user = await fetchUser(id);
  users.push(user);
}

while (queue.length > 0) {
  const item = queue.shift()!;
  await process(item);
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-await-in-loop': 'error'

docs.title: no-banned-types
docs.text: Ban ReturnType utility type and TypeScript indexed access types (T["key"]).

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
ReturnType<typeof fn> couples callers to the internal return type of a function rather than an explicit, named contract. When the function's return type changes, the inferred type silently changes everywhere it is used, bypassing deliberate type design.

Indexed access types (T["key"]) expose internal structure, making refactoring brittle and obscuring intent. Prefer explicit type aliases or interface members instead.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Declare an explicit return type
type FetchResult = { data: IUser; status: number };

async function fetchUser(): Promise<FetchResult> {
  // ...
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// ReturnType couples callers to implementation details
type MyReturnType = ReturnType<typeof fetchUser>;

// Indexed access exposes internal structure
type UserName = IUser['name'];

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-banned-types': 'error'

docs.title: no-dynamic-import
docs.text: Ban await import() and require() calls outside of test files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Dynamic imports and require() calls load modules at runtime rather than at bundle time. This hides dependencies from static analysis tools, prevents tree-shaking, complicates code-splitting strategies, and can introduce subtle load-order bugs. Static import declarations at the top of a file are always preferable in application code.

The rule is automatically skipped in test files (files matching *.test.{ts,js,tsx,jsx} or *.spec.{ts,js,tsx,jsx}), where dynamic loading is sometimes required by test utilities.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Static import at the top of the file
import { parseConfig } from './config-parser';

// Allowed in test files: mymodule.test.ts
const module = await import('./module');
const pkg = require('./package');

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect (in non-test files)
docs.text:
// In mymodule.ts
const module = await import('./heavy-module');

const pkg = require('some-package');

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-dynamic-import': 'error'

docs.title: no-empty-catch
docs.text: Disallow empty catch blocks that silently swallow errors.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: An empty catch block discards the error entirely. The caller has no indication that anything went wrong, diagnostics are impossible, and bugs become untraceable. Every caught error must be handled in some meaningful way: logged, re-thrown, returned as a result, or surfaced to the user.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
try {
  riskyOperation();
} catch (err) {
  logger.error('riskyOperation failed', err);
}

try {
  connectToDb();
} catch (err) {
  throw new Error(`Database connection failed: ${err}`);
}

try {
  cleanup();
} catch {
  throw new Error('Cleanup step failed');
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
try {
  riskyOperation();
} catch (err) {}

try {
  connectToDb();
} catch {}

try {
  cleanup();
} catch (err) {
} finally {
  teardown();
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-empty-catch': 'error'

docs.title: no-eslint-disable
docs.text: Prevent use of eslint-disable comments; fix the underlying issue instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
eslint-disable comments suppress rule violations rather than fixing them. They accumulate over time, making it impossible to know whether a rule violation is intentional or forgotten. A zero-tolerance codebase should have no suppression comments: every violation should be fixed at its source.

Flagged comment forms:

  • // eslint-disable
  • // eslint-disable-next-line
  • // eslint-disable-line
  • /* eslint-disable */ block comments

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Fix the underlying issue instead of disabling the rule
const MAX_SIZE = 100;
if (items.length > MAX_SIZE) {
  throw new Error('Too many items');
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// eslint-disable-next-line zero-tolerance/no-magic-numbers
if (items.length > 100) {
  /* ... */
}

/* eslint-disable zero-tolerance/no-throw-literal */
throw 'something went wrong';
/* eslint-enable zero-tolerance/no-throw-literal */

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-eslint-disable': 'error'

docs.title: no-export-alias
docs.text: Prevent use of aliases in export statements; export values under their original names.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Export aliases (export { foo as bar }) create a disconnect between the internal name of a value and the name that consumers use. This makes it harder to search for a symbol across the codebase and increases the chance of confusion. Export the value directly, or rename it at the declaration site.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
export { fetchUser };
export { createOrder };
export { validateSchema };

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
export { fetchUser as getUser };
export { createOrder as placeOrder };
export { validateSchema as checkSchema };

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-export-alias': 'error'

docs.title: no-identical-expressions
docs.text: Disallow identical expressions on both sides of a binary or logical operator (Sonar S1764).

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
When the left and right operands of a binary or logical expression are identical, the result is always deterministic — and almost certainly a copy-paste error. For example, a === a is always true, x || x is always x, and n - n is always 0.

Checked operators: ===, !==, ==, !=, &&, ||, ??, +, -, /, %

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
if (a === b) {
  /* ... */
}

const result = left || right;

const diff = endDate - startDate;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
if (value === value) {
  /* always true */
}

const x = obj || obj; // always obj

const zero = count - count; // always 0

if (user && user) {
  /* user is checked twice */
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-identical-expressions': 'error'

docs.title: no-jest-have-been-called
docs.text: Prohibit imprecise call-assertion matchers; use toHaveBeenCalledTimes and toHaveBeenNthCalledWith instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
toHaveBeenCalled / toBeCalled only asserts that a mock was called at least once without specifying how many times. toHaveBeenCalledWith / toBeCalledWith does not specify which invocation is being checked. toHaveBeenLastCalledWith / toLastCalledWith implicitly asserts only the final invocation, silently ignoring all others. All of these matchers can pass for the wrong reasons, producing false confidence.

Using toHaveBeenCalledTimes(n) forces you to declare the exact call count. Using toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(n, ...args) forces you to assert the arguments for a specific invocation. Together they produce precise, non-ambiguous test assertions.

Banned Replacement toHaveBeenCalled toHaveBeenCalledTimes toBeCalled toHaveBeenCalledTimes toHaveBeenCalledWith toHaveBeenNthCalledWith toBeCalledWith toHaveBeenNthCalledWith toHaveBeenLastCalledWith toHaveBeenNthCalledWith toLastCalledWith toHaveBeenNthCalledWith

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);

expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenNthCalledWith(1, 'expected-argument');

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenCalled();
expect(mockFn).toBeCalled();

expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenCalledWith('expected-argument');
expect(mockFn).toBeCalledWith('expected-argument');

expect(mockFn).toHaveBeenLastCalledWith('expected-argument');
expect(mockFn).toLastCalledWith('expected-argument');

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-jest-have-been-called': 'error'

docs.title: no-literal-unions
docs.text: Ban literal union types in favour of enums.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Literal union types ("active" | "inactive") scatter magic strings throughout the codebase, are not refactor-safe, and provide no single place to enumerate valid values. TypeScript enums (or const objects with as const) give the values a canonical home, making them easy to discover, iterate over, and refactor.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
enum Status {
  Active = 'active',
  Inactive = 'inactive',
}

enum Direction {
  North,
  South,
  East,
  West,
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
type Status = 'active' | 'inactive';

type Direction = 'north' | 'south' | 'east' | 'west';

type Size = 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg' | 'xl';

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
Pure boolean unions (true | false) are exempt because they represent the full boolean domain and do not benefit from an enum:

// ✅ Allowed — exhaustive boolean union
type Toggle = true | false;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-literal-unions': 'error'

docs.title: no-magic-numbers
docs.text: Disallow raw numeric literals in expressions; use named constants instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Magic numbers are numeric literals that appear without explanation. They make code hard to read (what does 86400 mean?) and hard to maintain (every occurrence must be updated if the value changes). Extracting them into named constants adds meaning and creates a single source of truth.

Allowed values: 0, 1, and -1 are universally understood and are always permitted.

Allowed locations:

  • const variable initialisers — this is how you define a named constant
  • TypeScript enum member values

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const SECONDS_PER_DAY = 86400;
const MAX_RETRIES = 3;
const HTTP_OK = 200;

// Allowed sentinel values
const index = -1;
const first = arr[0];
const length = arr.length + 1;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
setTimeout(callback, 86400 * 1000);

if (response.status === 200) {
  /* ... */
}

let retries = 3; // let or var declaration — not a named const

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-magic-numbers': 'error'

docs.title: no-magic-strings
docs.text: Disallow magic string literals in comparisons and switch-case clauses; use named constants instead.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
String literals used in comparisons (=== 'admin') or switch cases are magic strings: their meaning is implicit, they can be mis-typed silently, and every occurrence must be updated if the value changes. Defining them as named constants (const ROLE_ADMIN = 'admin') adds meaning and creates a single source of truth.

Checked locations:

  • ===, !==, ==, != binary comparisons
  • switch case test values

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const ROLE_ADMIN = 'admin';
const STATUS_ACTIVE = 'active';

if (user.role === ROLE_ADMIN) {
  /* ... */
}

switch (user.status) {
  case STATUS_ACTIVE:
    break;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
if (user.role === 'admin') {
  /* ... */
}

switch (user.status) {
  case 'active':
    break;
  case 'inactive':
    break;
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-magic-strings': 'error'

docs.title: no-mock-implementation
docs.text: Prohibit persistent mock setup methods; use their Once variants to prevent test bleeds.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
mockImplementation, mockReturnValue, mockResolvedValue, and mockRejectedValue configure a mock persistently for the lifetime of the test suite (or until manually reset). This means a mock configured in one test silently bleeds into subsequent tests, leading to hard-to-diagnose ordering-dependent failures.

The Once variants (mockImplementationOnce, mockReturnValueOnce, mockResolvedValueOnce, mockRejectedValueOnce) only apply for a single call and then revert. This eliminates the source of test bleeds entirely.

Banned Replacement mockImplementation mockImplementationOnce mockReturnValue mockReturnValueOnce mockResolvedValue mockResolvedValueOnce mockRejectedValue mockRejectedValueOnce

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
jest.fn().mockImplementationOnce(() => 'value');
jest.fn().mockReturnValueOnce(42);
jest.fn().mockResolvedValueOnce({ data: 'ok' });
jest.fn().mockRejectedValueOnce(new Error('failed'));

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
jest.fn().mockImplementation(() => 'value');
jest.fn().mockReturnValue(42);
jest.fn().mockResolvedValue({ data: 'ok' });
jest.fn().mockRejectedValue(new Error('failed'));

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-mock-implementation': 'error'

docs.title: no-non-null-assertion
docs.text: Disallow non-null assertions using the ! postfix operator.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: The ! postfix operator tells TypeScript to treat an expression as non-null without any runtime check. If the value is actually null or undefined at runtime, the assertion causes a crash rather than a handled error. Use optional chaining (?.), nullish coalescing (??), or an explicit null check instead.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Optional chaining
const name = user?.profile?.name;

// Nullish coalescing
const displayName = user?.name ?? 'Anonymous';

// Explicit null check
if (element !== null) {
  element.focus();
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const name = user!.profile!.name;

document.getElementById('root')!.innerHTML = '';

const value = maybeNull!;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-non-null-assertion': 'error'

docs.title: no-re-export
docs.text: Disallow re-export statements from parent or ancestor modules in non-barrel files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Re-export statements that reach outside the current directory (../) create upward dependencies that violate proper module hierarchy. Only barrel files (index.*) are exempt, as their sole purpose is to aggregate exports. All other files must not re-export from peer modules (../sibling) or ancestors (../../parent, ../../../grandparent).

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Direct exports
export { foo, bar };
export const value = 42;
export function myFunction() {}

// Re-exports from children (always allowed)
export { childFunction } from './child';
export * from './child/utils';

// In a barrel file (index.ts) — any re-export is allowed
// export { peerFunction } from '../sibling';
// export * from '../../parent';

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// Re-exports from peers — not allowed in non-barrel files
export { peerFunction } from '../sibling';
export * from '../utils';

// Re-exports from parents — not allowed
export { parentFunction } from '../../parent';
export * from '../../grandparent';

// Re-exports from grandparents — not allowed
export { grandparentFunction } from '../../../ancestor';
export * as ns from '../../../../distant-ancestor';

docs.title: no-redundant-boolean
docs.text: Disallow redundant comparisons to true or false (Sonar S1125).

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Comparing a boolean expression to true or false adds noise without adding meaning. isValid === true is identical to isValid; isActive !== false is identical to isActive. These redundant comparisons obscure intent and are commonly introduced by developers who are uncertain about the type of the value.

Checked operators: ===, !==

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
if (isValid) {
  /* ... */
}

if (!isActive) {
  /* ... */
}

return hasPermission;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
if (isValid === true) {
  /* ... */
}

if (isActive !== false) {
  /* ... */
}

return hasPermission === true;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-redundant-boolean': 'error'

docs.title: no-throw-literal
docs.text: Disallow throwing literals, plain objects, or template strings; always throw a new Error() instance.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type problem Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Only Error instances (and subclasses) include a stack trace. Throwing a string, object, number, or template literal discards that diagnostic information, making it impossible to trace where the error originated. Code that catches errors also often assumes it receives an Error object, so non-Error throws can cause secondary failures in error-handling paths.

Allowed throw arguments:

Node type Example NewExpression throw new Error('msg') Identifier throw err (re-throw a caught error) MemberExpression throw this.lastError CallExpression throw createError('msg') AwaitExpression throw await buildError()

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
throw new Error('something went wrong');

throw new TypeError('expected a string');

throw new RangeError('value out of range');

class ValidationError extends Error {}
throw new ValidationError('invalid input');

// Re-throwing a caught error
try {
  riskyOp();
} catch (err) {
  throw err;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
throw 'something went wrong';

throw 404;

throw { message: 'error', code: 500 };

throw `failed: ${reason}`;

throw condition ? 'error-a' : 'error-b';

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-throw-literal': 'error'

docs.title: no-type-assertion
docs.text: Prevent use of TypeScript as type assertions outside of test files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
as type assertions tell the TypeScript compiler to trust you over the type system. They are a common source of runtime errors because they bypass inference and structural checks. In production code, explicit type guards, generic functions, or proper data modelling should be used instead.

The rule allows as unknown in test files (.test.*, .spec.*) because it is a necessary pattern in rule testers and other test utilities.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// Use a type guard instead
function isUser(value: unknown): value is IUser {
  return typeof value === 'object' && value !== null && 'name' in value && 'email' in value;
}

// Generic function with a constraint
function first<T>(arr: T[]): T | undefined {
  return arr[0];
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const user = response.data as IUser;

const element = document.querySelector('#app') as HTMLDivElement;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/no-type-assertion': 'error'

docs.title: require-interface-prefix
docs.text: Enforce that TypeScript interface names start with I (capital letter) followed by an uppercase character.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Prefixing interface names with I makes it immediately clear in code that a type is an interface rather than a class or type alias. This convention improves readability, especially in large codebases where interfaces are heavily used.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
interface IUser {
  name: string;
  email: string;
}

interface IRepository<T> {
  findById(id: string): Promise<T>;
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
interface User {
  name: string;
}

interface userProfile {
  age: number;
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options. Enable it in your ESLint config:

'zero-tolerance/require-interface-prefix': 'error'

docs.title: require-jsdoc-functions
docs.text: Require JSDoc documentation comments on all function declarations, function expressions, and arrow functions outside of test files.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: JSDoc comments are essential for self-documenting code and support IDE tooling, generated API documentation, and clear intent communication to future maintainers. This rule is automatically skipped in test files (.test.* / .spec.*).

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
/**
 * Fetches a user record by ID.
 */
async function fetchUser(id: string): Promise<IUser> {
  return db.users.findById(id);
}

/**
 * Validates that the given email address is well-formed.
 */
const isValidEmail = (email: string): boolean => /^[^@]+@[^@]+$/.test(email);

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
async function fetchUser(id: string): Promise<IUser> {
  return db.users.findById(id);
}

const isValidEmail = (email: string): boolean => /^[^@]+@[^@]+$/.test(email);

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-jsdoc-functions': 'error'

docs.title: Test files are exempt
docs.text: Functions inside files matching *.test.ts, *.spec.ts, *.test.js, *.spec.js (and their JSX equivalents) are not checked.

docs.title: require-optional-chaining
docs.text: Require optional chaining instead of repeated logical guard access.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Patterns like obj && obj.value or fn && fn() duplicate the same guard expression and make code harder to read. Optional chaining expresses the same intent directly in one expression and removes repetition.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const profile = user?.profile;
const result = fn?.();
const name = account?.owner?.name;

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const profile = user && user.profile;
const result = fn && fn();
const name = account.owner && account.owner.name;

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-optional-chaining': 'error'

docs.title: require-test-description-style
docs.text: Enforce that it() and test() descriptions start with the word should.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Test descriptions starting with should form a consistent, human-readable specification language. Reading them aloud reveals the intended behaviour: "it should render correctly", "it should throw when the input is invalid". This convention makes test reports self-documenting and helps reviewers scan failures quickly.

The rule applies to it(), test(), it.only(), and test.only(). It deliberately skips it.skip() and test.skip() to avoid friction when temporarily disabling tests.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
it('should render correctly', () => {});

test('should throw when input is invalid', () => {});

it.only('should handle concurrent requests', () => {});

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
it('renders correctly', () => {});

test('handles errors', () => {});

it('Check that user is saved', () => {});

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-test-description-style': 'error'

docs.title: require-zod-schema-description
docs.text: Enforce that Zod schema variable initialisers include a .describe() call.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Zod's .describe() method attaches a human-readable description to a schema. This description is used by tools that generate JSON Schema, OpenAPI specs, and documentation from Zod schemas. Without .describe() the generated output lacks meaningful field descriptions.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
const nameSchema = z.string().describe('The user's display name');

const userSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string(),
  age:  z.number(),
}).describe('A registered user');

const statusSchema = z.enum(['active', 'inactive']).describe('Account status');

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
const nameSchema = z.string();

const userSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string(),
  age: z.number(),
});

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/require-zod-schema-description': 'warn'

docs.title: sort-functions
docs.text: Require top-level function declarations to be sorted alphabetically by name.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable No Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: Alphabetically ordered top-level functions make a module's API easy to navigate and reduce the chance of duplicate function definitions. The check is case-insensitive and applies to both function declarations and const variable declarations that initialise arrow functions or function expressions at the top level of the program. Methods, nested functions, and non-const variable declarations are not checked.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
function buildReport() {
  /* ... */
}
function fetchData() {
  /* ... */
}
function validateInput() {
  /* ... */
}

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
function validateInput() {
  /* ... */
}
function buildReport() {
  /* ... */
} // should come before validateInput
function fetchData() {
  /* ... */
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/sort-functions': 'error'

docs.title: sort-imports
docs.text: Require import declarations to be grouped and sorted: side-effect → external → parent → peer → index, with alphabetical ordering within each group.

docs.title: Rule Details
docs.text: Property Value Type suggestion Fixable Yes (code) Recommended warn Strict error

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
Consistent import ordering reduces merge conflicts and makes it immediately clear where a dependency comes from. Imports must appear in five groups in order — side-effect (bare) imports, external packages, parent-directory (../) imports, peer (./) imports, and the index (.) import — with each group sorted alphabetically (case-insensitive).

Side-effect imports (import 'module') have no specifiers and often set up runtime prerequisites such as polyfills or reflection metadata. They must appear before all other imports.

docs.title: ✅ Correct
docs.text:
// 1. Side-effect imports — alphabetical
import 'reflect-metadata';

// 2. External packages — alphabetical
import express from 'express';
import { injectable } from 'inversify';
import path from 'path';

// 3. Parent imports — alphabetical
import { models } from '../models';
import { utils } from '../utils';

// 4. Peer imports — alphabetical
import { auth } from './auth';
import { users } from './users';

// 5. Index import
import self from '.';

docs.title: ❌ Incorrect
docs.text:
// External import placed before side-effect import — wrong group order
import { injectable } from 'inversify';
import 'reflect-metadata';

// Peer import placed before external — wrong group order
import { auth } from './auth';
import express from 'express';

// Parent import placed before external — wrong group order
import { utils } from '../utils';
import lodash from 'lodash';

// External packages out of alphabetical order
import zlib from 'zlib';
import axios from 'axios'; // 'axios' should come before 'zlib'

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
This rule has no options:

'zero-tolerance/sort-imports': 'error'
